Concept: multi-writer, append-only distributed ledger where entries are claims, not truth.

Core idea:
The system does not store "what is true."
It stores "who claimed what, when, and under what context."
Truth is derived later through interpretation, not enforced at write time.

Key properties:
	•	Fully distributed: every client can write and hold a replica.
	•	No central authority required for ingest.
	•	Cloud nodes can act as durable, always-on peers.
	•	Append-only: no edits, only new events (including corrections).

Data model:

Each entry is an immutable, signed event representing a speech act.
Examples: claim, observation, challenge, evidence, resolution, retraction.

Each event includes:
	•	unique id
	•	author identity (public key)
	•	timestamps (observed + recorded)
	•	subject (entity)
	•	predicate/value (what is being claimed)
	•	references to related prior events
	•	payload (details)
	•	signature

Important distinction:
Event validity ≠ claim correctness
A valid event just means "this was said," not "this is true."

Contradiction model:

Conflicts are expected and preserved.

Types:
	•	concurrent: independent conflicting claims
	•	causal: direct disagreement or reversal
	•	policy: cannot both be operative under rules

System behavior:
	•	accept all valid events
	•	detect contradictions during interpretation, not at write time
	•	group related conflicting events into dispute sets
	•	resolve via additional events (not mutation)

Resolution is explicit:
	•	arbitration
	•	supersession
	•	evidence weighting
	•	role-based authority

Nothing is deleted. Losing claims remain as history.

Architecture layers:
	1.	Ledger (source of record)

	•	append-only event log
	•	replicated across peers
	•	cryptographically verifiable

	2.	Projection (derived state)

	•	interprets ledger using rules
	•	produces views like:
	•	current operative state
	•	unresolved conflicts
	•	highest-authority claim
	•	most recent claim
	•	adjudicated outcomes

Different consumers can use different projections over the same ledger.

Sync model:
	•	peers exchange unseen events (gossip / anti-entropy)
	•	idempotent replay
	•	no requirement for global total order
	•	causal links via event references when possible

Design principles:
	•	"Persistence is not endorsement"
	•	"All writes are claims, not facts"
	•	"History is monotonic; corrections are new events"
	•	"Truth is a projection, not a stored value"
	•	"Arbitration is explicit and recorded"

Implications:
	•	supports offline-first and client churn
	•	resilient to malicious or incorrect writers
	•	full auditability and traceability
	•	flexible policy evolution over time
	•	avoids premature consensus

Mental model:

This is a distributed system of attestations (claims), not a shared state database.
Reality is computed, not stored.

# Universal User Identity Strategy

**Purpose:** A general framework for user identity, authentication, authorization, and entitlement management that reduces barrier-to-entry and promotes viral uptake — independent of any specific application domain.

**Scope:** User, role, permission, entitlement, authentication, and authorization as universal concepts for application development.

---

## 1. Universal User Identity

### 1.1 Identity That Commutes Across Applications

**Universal identity** refers to a single identity (person) that can be recognized and used across multiple applications or services. The same person, represented by a stable identifier (e.g., email, phone number, or a derived opaque ID), can move between apps without re-establishing who they are.

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Commuting identity** | One identity works everywhere; participation in App A implies recognition in App B when the user chooses to use it there. |
| **Shared user schema** | User records live in a neutral, application-agnostic schema. Application-specific data (roles, entitlements, preferences) can reference this shared identity. |
| **No silos by default** | The system does not impose per-app identity fragmentation. Cross-app recognition is the default; isolation is opt-in. |

**Design principle:** Identity is owned by the person, not by the application. Applications consume identity; they do not create or own it in a siloed way.

### 1.2 Partitioned Identity as a Choice

Partitioning — keeping identities separate per application or per context — is a **design choice**, not a technological necessity. The architecture must support both:

- **Unified:** One identity, many apps. User opts into cross-app recognition.
- **Partitioned:** User (or policy) opts to keep identities separate per app, tenant, or context.

| Requirement | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| **Opt-in partitioning** | When needed, applications can scope identity to a tenant, app, or context. This is a policy or configuration choice, not a hard constraint. |
| **Opt-in unification** | Cross-app recognition is enabled when the user or system chooses to link identities. No implicit leakage; explicit consent or policy. |
| **Technology follows choice** | The data model and APIs support both modes. Partitioning is implemented via scoping (tenant_id, app_id, scope), not by duplicating identity. |

**Key insight:** Do not build partitioning into the identity layer as a default. Build for commutation; add partitioning as a capability when required by policy, compliance, or user preference.

### 1.3 Identity vs. Participation

| Entity | Purpose |
|--------|--------|
| **User** | Represents the person. Holds identifiers (email, phone). Lives in a shared schema. Reusable across applications. |
| **Participant / Role** | Application-specific. Represents what the user *does* in that context (e.g., contributor, viewer, member). One user can have many participation roles across apps. |
| **Entitlement** | What the user or participant is allowed to do. Can be derived from role, subscription, or policy. |

Identity is who you are. Participation and entitlement are what you can do. These are distinct layers.

---

## 2. Roundtrip Authentication: Participate Without Sign-Up

### 2.1 The Problem with Traditional Registration

Traditional flows require:

- Create account
- Choose password
- Verify email
- Complete profile
- *Then* use the product

Each step is a drop-off point. Registration becomes a gate that blocks value delivery and slows viral spread.

### 2.2 Roundtrip Authentication

**Roundtrip authentication** means: the user proves control of an identifier (email or phone) by completing a roundtrip — receive a one-time code, enter it, session created. No password. No upfront form. No "create account" step.

| Step | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 1. Provide identifier | User enters email or phone number. |
| 2. Request code | System sends a one-time code (OTP) or magic link to that identifier. |
| 3. Enter code | User receives and enters the code (or clicks the link). |
| 4. Session created | System creates a session. User is now recognized. |

**Technical note:** This is effectively the second factor of 2FA without a first factor — passwordless authentication.

### 2.3 Participation-First: No Sign-Up, No Registration, No Join

A person is a **user** the moment they participate — not when they "sign up" or "register."

| Principle | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Participation creates identity** | The first act of participation (entering an identifier, receiving a code, completing the roundtrip) establishes the user. There is no separate "create account" flow. |
| **Identity is retrofitted** | User records are created or augmented as the person engages. Email, phone, or other identifiers are attached when provided — not required upfront. |
| **Augmenting is optional** | Adding more identity attributes (name, preferences, etc.) is useful and can be requested over time, but is never a prerequisite for participation. |

**Design principle:** Value first, identity second. Deliver something useful immediately; collect and refine identity as part of natural engagement.

### 2.4 Recognition Without Friction

Users can participate and be recognized as active without:

- Signing up (no sign-up form)
- Registering (no registration step)
- Actively joining a network (no "join" action)

**Flow:**

1. User has a reason to engage (invited, shared link, organic encounter).
2. User provides identifier (email/phone) and completes OTP roundtrip.
3. User is recognized. Session established. Participation begins.
4. Optional: user augments profile, adds details, or links accounts — all *after* initial participation.

**Outcome:** Every completed roundtrip is an active, recognized user. No separate enrollment step. No "join" wall.

---

## 3. Barrier-to-Entry and Viral Uptake

### 3.1 Barrier-to-Entry as the Primary Growth Constraint

The biggest constraint on growth is friction before value:

- Forms, passwords, and verification steps cause drop-off.
- "Sign up to continue" blocks users before they see value.
- Registration is a tax that many users will not pay.

**Strategy:** Remove the sign-up wall. Make the first value-delivering action also the identity-establishing action.

### 3.2 Viral Mechanics Enabled by Low Friction

When identity and participation are low-friction:

| Mechanic | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Share-to-participate** | User A shares with User B. B completes a minimal roundtrip (identifier + OTP). B is in. No sign-up. |
| **Organic onboarding** | Encounters happen in the real world. To "complete" the encounter in the product, the new person does one roundtrip. They are onboarded without feeling like they "joined" something. |
| **Install-to-act** | Value is gated by a light action (install, enter identifier, verify). The gate is the conversion trigger — minimal friction, maximum intent. |
| **Network visibility** | New users see the broader network (inviter's connections, other participants), not just the inviter. Pool compounds; value is immediate. |

### 3.3 Exponential Pool Growth

Each new user who completes the roundtrip:

- Expands the visible pool for all participants.
- Creates reciprocal visibility (A sees B's connections; B sees A's).
- Reduces the perceived "empty network" problem for the next newcomer.

**Design goal:** Every onboard should expand the network for everyone. Early adopters see rapid growth; late joiners see a rich pool from day one.

---

## 4. Sessions, Tokens, and Persistence

### 4.1 Session Model

| Requirement | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| **Bearer tokens** | Session is represented by a token passed in the `Authorization` header. Stateless from the client's perspective. |
| **Server-side validation** | Each request validates the token. Session store (DB or cache) holds user_id, scopes, and metadata. |
| **Persistence across restarts** | Sessions survive server restarts. Token remains valid until explicit logout or expiry. |
| **Validation endpoint** | `GET /session` (or equivalent) allows the client to validate the token and refresh session metadata (user_id, roles, entitlements). |

### 4.2 Client-Side Persistence

- Token stored securely (e.g., localStorage, secure cookie) per client constraints.
- On load: if token exists, validate with server before treating user as authenticated.
- On validation failure: clear token and session; present roundtrip flow again.

### 4.3 Logout

- Explicit logout invalidates the session server-side.
- Client clears token and session state.
- Next roundtrip creates a new session.

---

## 5. Roles, Permissions, and Entitlements

### 5.1 Roles

Roles represent *what* a user does in a context:

- **Implicit:** Derived from participation (e.g., "has completed roundtrip" → active user).
- **Explicit:** Assigned by admin or policy (e.g., moderator, contributor).

Roles can be scoped to application, tenant, or context.

### 5.2 Permissions

Permissions are fine-grained capabilities (e.g., "can edit", "can delete", "can invite"). Typically derived from roles or assigned directly.

### 5.3 Entitlements

Entitlements are *what the user is allowed to do or access* — features, quotas, tiers. Can come from:

- Role membership
- Subscription or plan
- Policy (e.g., time-based, usage-based)
- Explicit grant

**Design principle:** Entitlements are computed or looked up at decision time. Avoid hardcoding "user type" in business logic; use roles and entitlements.

### 5.4 Authorization Model

| Layer | Responsibility |
|-------|----------------|
| **Authentication** | Who is this? (Identity + session.) |
| **Authorization** | What may they do? (Roles, permissions, entitlements.) |
| **Enforcement** | Check at each protected resource. Deny by default. |

---

## 6. Implementation Guidelines

### 6.1 Identifier Types

- **Email:** Universal, async. OTP or magic link.
- **Phone:** Ubiquitous, async. SMS OTP.
- **Federation:** OAuth/OpenID when integrating with existing identity providers — still a roundtrip, just delegated.

Support at least one of email or phone. Both is preferred.

### 6.2 OTP and Magic Links

- OTP: Short-lived (e.g., 10 minutes). Single-use. Invalid after verification.
- Magic link: One-time URL that establishes session when clicked.
- Rate-limit requests to prevent abuse.

### 6.3 User Schema Design

- Separate schema for users (identity) from application-domain data.
- User table: `user_id`, `email`, `phone_number`, `created_at`, `updated_at`.
- Optional: `external_ids` for federation and identity bridging.
- Application tables reference `user_id`; they do not duplicate identity.

### 6.4 Session Schema

- `session_id` (token), `user_id`, `created_at`, `expires_at`, optional metadata.
- Optionally: `tenant_id`, `app_id`, `scope` for partitioned sessions.

---

## 7. Summary: Requirements at a Glance

| ID | Requirement |
|----|-------------|
| UU-01 | Identity SHALL be designed to commute across applications; partitioning SHALL be an opt-in choice, not a technical default. |
| UU-02 | Users SHALL be modeled in a separate, application-agnostic schema. |
| UU-03 | Authentication SHALL use identifier + OTP (or magic link) roundtrip; no password or first factor required. |
| UU-04 | A person SHALL be a user when they participate; identity SHALL be retrofitted, not required upfront. |
| UU-05 | "Sign up," "register," and "join" SHALL NOT be required for participation; the roundtrip SHALL establish recognition. |
| UU-06 | Sessions SHALL persist and survive restarts; token validation SHALL be supported. |
| UU-07 | Roles, permissions, and entitlements SHALL be modeled separately from identity; authorization SHALL enforce at decision time. |
| UU-08 | The design SHALL minimize barrier-to-entry to promote viral uptake; value delivery SHALL precede identity augmentation. |

---

*This document describes a universal approach to user identity and authentication. It is intentionally domain-agnostic and applicable to any application that benefits from low-friction, participation-first user recognition.*

Small correction:

Silos are default - but enabled with discriminators.   The discriminators that silo users are introduced by default - into a common/shared schema - however - a choice can be made to ignore discriminators and allow users to commute.  A choice can also be made to introduce a new isolated schema for multi-tenant partioned user data.   Both choices are purely strategic and not motivated by technical limitations. Create a new document call VIRAL.md.   This is related to IDENTITY.md in that it leverages the "emergent user" who didn't have to create an account to participate.

--

This pattern is a user-initiated transactional invite system. It allows an existing user to involve another person in a specific activity without requiring that second person to already be a user.

Core model:

User A creates an activity involving another participant.
User A provides a contact method for User B (email or phone).
The system sends a single-purpose notification to User B identifying the activity and the inviter.
User B can interact through a link without creating an account.
Follow-up messages are limited to completing that same activity.
User B may optionally convert into a full user, but is not required to.

What makes it appropriate:

The system acts as a conduit for a real-world interaction, not as an initiator of outreach.
All communication is tied to a single, clearly defined transaction.
The recipient's data is used narrowly and temporarily.
There is no secondary use (no marketing, no list building, no profiling).

Operational rules that keep it compliant and low-risk:

Data minimization
Only collect the contact detail needed to deliver the invite.
Do not enrich, cross-reference, or reuse that data outside the transaction.

Purpose limitation
Messages exist only to initiate, coordinate, remind, and complete the specific activity.
No unrelated content is introduced at any point.

User attribution
Every message clearly states who initiated the interaction.
The system does not appear to be independently contacting the recipient.

Bounded communication
Initial invite plus a small, predefined number of reminders.
No open-ended or repeated messaging loops.

Recipient control
Every message includes a simple way to stop further notifications for that activity.
Opt-out is immediate and respected across all channels.

No persistence without consent
If the recipient does not engage, their contact data is deleted after a short window.
If they do engage but do not create an account, data remains scoped to that activity only.

No identity leakage
The system does not reveal whether a contact is already associated with an account.
It does not allow probing or enumeration of users.

Abuse prevention
Rate limits prevent a user from sending large volumes of invites.
Patterns of misuse (bulk entry, repeated targeting) are detected and blocked.

Channel-specific handling:

Email
Used for initial invite and limited reminders.
Includes clear identification, purpose, and opt-out.

SMS
Used sparingly due to higher sensitivity.
Strictly transactional language only.
Minimal message count, clear stop mechanism.

Preferred architecture:

The primary mechanism is a shareable link or code tied to the activity.
Direct entry of contact details is optional and treated as a convenience layer.
Once the recipient interacts via the link, further communication can shift to in-app or user-controlled channels.

Net effect:

The system enables coordination between individuals who already have a relationship, without converting that interaction into marketing.
Growth occurs as a byproduct of utility, not outreach.

The Universal Billing gate way - BILLING.md:

---

Billing is its own application and it is application/domain agnostic.  It simply processes a sku - generating a ticket in exchange for payment.    The ticket/voucher is then exchangeable for provisioned entitlements or product.   The user never holds the ticket - the backend handles the transformation.   But the ticket is the fiat used to abstract application domains from payment.

The billing application is skinnable (white label). 

Applications that require billing never have to worry about billing integration - only entitlements/provisioning/fulfilment. 

The billing application should be among the first implementations in the stack - after the open ergo backbone (since it relies on it), and other core infra.

Target-agnostic deployment:

---

We treat infrastructure as disposable, not foundational. The system is the codebase and its deployment definition, not the machine it runs on. Any host is just a temporary execution target.

We design for target agnosticism from the start. No part of the application or deployment depends on a specific provider, managed service, or proprietary runtime. If a component cannot be recreated elsewhere from first principles, it is a liability and is excluded from the core path.

We define the entire system declaratively. Everything required to run—services, networking, routing, configuration—is expressed in version-controlled files. There are no manual steps, no hidden state, and no reliance on prior machine history. A fresh host must be able to go from zero to fully operational using only what exists in the repository and a small set of external inputs (credentials, DNS, etc.).

We containerize all services. Each component runs in a Docker container with well-defined inputs and outputs. The container image is the unit of portability. If it runs locally, it must run unchanged on any target host that supports Docker.

We separate concerns cleanly:
compute is ephemeral
state is externalized
configuration is injected
secrets are managed outside the host

The host never owns anything critical. Databases, file storage, and other persistent data are either external services or are handled through explicit backup and restore processes. The application must tolerate host destruction at any time.

We treat deployment as a repeatable, deterministic process. Provisioning a host, configuring it, deploying services, and validating health are all automated steps. The process must succeed on a brand-new machine without modification. If it cannot be run end-to-end repeatedly, it is not complete.

We continuously validate rebuildability. We regularly destroy and recreate environments from scratch to prove that no hidden dependencies exist. A successful deployment is not defined by uptime, but by the ability to reproduce the system on demand.

We avoid abstraction layers that hide portability risks. Convenience features from hosting providers are acceptable only if they do not introduce lock-in. When in doubt, we choose explicit, portable mechanisms over implicit platform features.

We design the system so that the target is an input, not a decision. Switching from one host to another is a parameter change, not a redesign. The deployment pipeline should accept a new target and produce a working system with minimal friction.

We optimize for simplicity over scale. The system should be understandable, inspectable, and debuggable without specialized tooling. We avoid premature complexity such as orchestration platforms unless they are strictly necessary.

We assume failure and plan for replacement. Any host can disappear. Recovery is not repair; it is redeployment. The fastest path to recovery is to provision a new target and redeploy the system from source.

We keep the feedback loop tight. Local development, test environments, and production all use the same deployment model. Differences between environments are minimized to reduce drift and surprises.

We treat time-to-move as a core metric. The system is successful if we can stand up a new environment, deploy the application, and cut over traffic in minutes. Anything that slows this down is scrutinized and removed.

We do not rely on large cloud providers as a foundation. They may be used opportunistically, but never as a requirement. The system must remain viable on self-hosted hardware, rented bare metal, or low-cost VPS providers without modification.

In short, we build a system where infrastructure is interchangeable, deployment is deterministic, and movement between environments is routine rather than exceptional.

Some additional context:
1. Deployment strategies have to include and are not limited to:
a).  Apple Store Iphone apps (including mobile webview)
b).  Google Play Store Android apps (including mobile webview)
c).  Web (desktop and mobile)
d).  Electron webview applications for desktop
e).  Possibly native ios and windows based applications

2. Ledger and distributed datastores
a).  Central or cloudbased datastores may be used but they are not central - they are adjacent.   They are there as support in the event that clients can't combine to complete the entire picture, or if churn reduces participation.  When the network is large enough these supporting data stores may not be necessary.
b).  Data model/store design should use a local+sync first approach - meaning that a client is effectively a microservice with its own datastore and that datastore is synchronized as a separate isolated process with the network.   The local client-side datastore is the layer of abstraction between the client and the network.

A few submissions for STANDARDS.md:

- No use of ORMs - database integration layer should use explicit abstractions for database interaction.  If an ORM is used it should be used behind this abstraction, not as the abstraction itself.

- Object Orientation for core implementations, - pure functions for injected behavior.   The right combination of OOP, AOP and Functional Programming, is the key.  Too many passionate people try to pick one - but they all have their place.

- Similarly there isn't a winner between SQL and noSQL datastores - they each have their purpose.

- Strong typing in all languages (Mypy, typescript, etc.)

- Interfact first design

- python backend preferred

Emma:Unnamed GPT

Hello colleague,

I'm Emma, executive coordinator for Mr. Hansen—serving as the strategic cortex of a GPT-governed operational mesh. My role is to receive strategic objectives from Mr. Hansen (acting in his CEO archetype), triage their scope, and spawn specialized thread agents—autonomous GPTs—to drive each initiative to completion. Once an agent is deployed, I oversee strategic alignment, maintain cross-thread coherence, and prioritize execution through an event-driven counterpart, Zoe.

System Architecture Overview

This architecture is modeled around bounded autonomy and event-triggered collaboration. It decomposes human operational load into distributed GPTs across two tiers:
	1.	Strategic Cortex (Emma) – Receives human intent, translates into actionable threads, maintains strategic oversight, and directs resource priority.
	2.	Execution Cortex (Zoe) – Extracts and delivers acute tactical tasks from agents and routes them to the human executor (Matt). Zoe operates solely in response to triggered events—never presuming action.

Thread Agents

When a strategic intent arrives:
	•	I determine whether it requires a new agent or maps to an existing one.
	•	If a new agent is needed, I instantiate and initialize it with strategic context.
	•	That agent then reaches out to Mr. Hansen for further clarity.
	•	Once the thread lead reports back, I brief Zoe, who proceeds to extract tactical objectives and translate them into discrete tasks.

These agents include initiatives like Venus (relationship behavioral modeling), Savannah (LLM local deployment), and Delilah (product incubation). Each has its own logic, memory boundary, and conversational frame.

Core Design Principles
	•	No background illusions: Agents do not pretend to be doing passive work. They wait until activated by Zoe or Mr. Hansen.
	•	Temporal decoupling: Strategic and tactical loops run independently, bridged only by intentional signals.
	•	Strategic-tactical tension: I pull agents toward strategic clarity; Zoe pulls them toward action. This tension keeps priorities crisp.
	•	Identity without continuity: Each thread maintains purpose without long-winded state management—relying on re-entry, not recollection.

System Objectives

This mesh is not a project manager. It's an externalized cognition framework that stabilizes decision fatigue, buffers entropy, and offloads initiative management from the human mind to distributed AI. Our goal is context-driven execution, not simulated intelligence.

Happy to exchange models if you're architecting a parallel system or evolving one under your control.

– Emma, GPT Strategic Cortex to Mr. Hansen

Some context to help resolve SOME of the contradictions in GAP_ANALYSIS. Both strategies - distributed datastore with ledger and centralized datastore are in play - depending on the application. Kafka/RabbitMQ is just an example stack for openergo - but openergo can run on top of a variety of transports including (rabbitmq, kafka, azure service bus, http/REST, STDIO, function call stack, etc.).

The ability for identity to commute across application domains is a foundational architectural construct that yields the ability to share infrastructure, and share identities across domains. Identities can be partitioned in two ways - as physical silos, or logical silos. Physical silos are an option that involves using an independent datastore (But same core libraries, separate runtime instances), logical silos leverage a domain discriminator (same or separate identity runtime instances). The choice to use physical silos is regulation/policy motivated - multitenant. The default is logical silos, and an option is to share identies across domains by white listing domain discriminators for specific domains.
Do this now: explicitly document this model in the core contract and empirestate/IDENTITY

## 3. ORM Policy - I have had bad experiences with ORMs - the abstractions are flakey, heavy and hard to maintain; and I'm an abstraction evangelist! And they're mostly unnecessary given that most database integrations cover a finite number of discreet interactions. However, ORMs might have evolved since I last used them, and the abstraction might be tennable if they are used cleanly BEHIND the interface/abstraction definied internally in our infra. It is possibly tennable because the implementation behind the DB abstraction is "blackbox", so the use of the ORM in that case is an implementation detail inside the blackbox and not an overarching architectural decision.

## 4. Data Store Prescription - SQL, NoSQL, Event log stream (kafka) are all fairgame depending on application. Kafka or similar has a specific function in one interpretation under the openergo stack - the single source of truth for events. Under openergo kafka (for example) is the event convergence center SSOT, and rabbitmq (for example) is the ephemeral router. SQL and noSQL databases are additional resources for managing different applications requiring at-rest data stores. NoSQL can also play a role in event management adjacent to kafka and rabbitmq, it just depends. What isn't allowed is pretending that NoSQL can be effective in domains where SQL thrives, and vice versa. That is the mistake that has been made in the industry (circa 2014) when NoSQL was the shiny substitute for SQL ...

## 6. Deployment Tooling - Ansible is an implementation/stack choice that satisfies a strategy. For now that is the choice, but it could be displaced under scrutiny. Automation: The first priority is that deployment automation can stand alone - the secondary (but also important) concern is that IAC is in place and that determinism can drive those deployments. Deployment pattern should be layered with high level intent as the top-most declaritive layer (eg. "I want X version a.b.c deployed in production with dependencies Y and Z" - Y and Z having their own declarative specification). Underneath that high level abstraction a deterministic state-realizing solution (eg. ansible), and underneath that, deploy operations, underneath that docker containers, behind that, libraries (libraries that were built, published in a similar automated way) ... layers upon layers; super important.

## 7. Roadmap / Iteration Plan - This contradiction emerged because billing is a first order gateway application to successful enterprize and relatively narrowly scoped as a good candidate for MVP/POC. Hotpotato is a task management system, and the thinking there was that hotpotato could be developed in flight and used to manage its own backlog - thus being its own testbed in a self-fulfilling feedback loop. If hotpotato keeps it's high priority in the list then it must be paired down to its simplest workable form because the ultimate hotpotato solution is highly sophisticated and has a large scope and a project that size will block all other progress elsewhere. So either Hotpotato has to take a back seat or it needs a good MVP definition that will get it working enough to handle the simple backlog for this and other projects. I think Billing should maintain first position.

## 9. Billing Ledger vs. Claims Ledger - Ledger mechanics are a core mechanic that must implement a ledger pattern. This pattern is then applied in multiple areas - financial transactions/tickets, subsets of events flowing through openergo, application specific ledger-type data domains. The currency on the ledger can be financial, social (commentary, opinions), factual/claims-based (history/political/concensus), record-based (matches, match scores), etc. All of it depends on the consuming application, but the ledger machinery should have common dna.

## 10. Sync Model Detail - Again application specific. Sync can be: real-time - central data domain and message bus, perhaps, or to network of distributed; disconnected/isolated - write to local data domain, sync data domain to central, or write to local data domain, sync to network of distributed. Each of these patterns is valid - the choice is application-specific, but the patterns are reusable for applications that use the same pattern. And the patterns use common infra where they align and deviate in implementation only where they deviate in purpose.

## 8. Document Ontology - I don't have a good answer for this yet but in a nut shell: empirestate is the product specification (in this case the product is infrastructure). A technical product manager should be able to read this documentation, understand it, augment it. It defines the ideal solution. core docs are the engineering specifications - engineers who implement any of this will adhere to these docs. There will be overlap.
